items.prompt_md: In `combination_sum`, why does the recursive call use `backtrack(i, remaining - candidates[i])` instead of `backtrack(i + 1, ...)`?
options: Because the same candidate can be reused, so the next choice is allowed to start at the same index i again
Because it's required to reach the base case at all
Because sorting shifts every index over by one position
Because it makes the running sum compute faster
items.answer: Because the same candidate can be reused, so the next choice is allowed to start at the same index i again
items.explanation_md: Passing `i` (not `i + 1`) forward keeps `candidates[i]` eligible for the very next pick, which is exactly what lets a value like 2 be used multiple times in one combination.

items.prompt_md: You must list every combination of numbers, allowing reuse, that add up to exactly a target amount. Which approach fits?
options: Backtrack with a running sum: choose a candidate, recurse (reuse allowed), un-choose, and prune once the sorted candidates exceed what's left
Two pointers converging on a sorted array
A hash map of prefix sums seen so far
Binary search for the target inside a sorted array
items.answer: Backtrack with a running sum: choose a candidate, recurse (reuse allowed), un-choose, and prune once the sorted candidates exceed what's left
items.explanation_md: "Every combination summing to a target, reuse allowed" is the fingerprint for backtracking with a running-sum prune — there's no fixed pair or window to exploit, just an exhaustive search that sorting lets you cut short.

items.prompt_md: What best describes the time complexity of the combination-sum backtracking search in the worst case?
options: O(2^target) — exponential in the worst case
items.answer: O(2^target) — exponential in the worst case
items.explanation_md: The search tree's branching depends on how many times candidates can be reused before hitting the target. Pruning cuts a lot of branches in practice, but the worst case (e.g. many small candidates) is still exponential in the target, not polynomial.
